name: Cross-basin generalization — single-basin transfer + gradual addition
description: Trained separate CycloneUFNO models per basin (WP, NA, EP) and tested each on all three basins. Also trained with gradual basin addition (WP → WP+NA → WP+NA+EP) to measure data pooling effect.

observations.generalisation: The model generalises well across basins. All transfer RMSE values fall in a tight range (0.084–0.130), with off-diagonal values barely worse than diagonal. This suggests the model is learning generalisable storm decay physics rather than basin-specific patterns.
observations.best_source_basin: NA training data is disproportionately informative. A model trained on NA alone transfers to WP (0.104) and EP (0.091) better than WP-trained or EP-trained models transfer to those same basins. NA storms appear to carry the most representative signal across all three ocean basins.
observations.ep_limitation: EP-trained model has the worst WP transfer (0.130), expected given EP has the fewest training samples. Training on a small, less diverse basin limits generalisation.
observations.gradual_addition: Adding NA to WP training reduces NA and EP test RMSE noticeably. Adding EP on top of WP+NA has minimal effect — EP data provides little additional signal beyond what WP+NA already captures.
observations.wp_pooling_cost: WP test RMSE rises slightly as more basins are added (0.113 → 0.111), suggesting model capacity is being spread across basins and WP suffers marginally from the competition.
observations.all_basin_model: The all-basin (WP+NA+EP) model is consistently middle-of-the-road — never the best for any individual basin but the most robust overall choice if a single model is needed.
observations.policy_learning_link: Strong cross-basin transfer supports the hypothesis that the model learns generalisable atmospheric dynamics. This is a prerequisite for policy learning / generalisation across environments — the same physical patterns (pressure-wind coupling, intensity trends) drive decay across all basins.
